coredata.dc:title: Defects-rich graphene/carbon quantum dot composites as highly efficient electrocatalysts for aqueous zinc/air batteries

coredata.dc:description: The development of metal-free electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has great influence on many energy conversion devices. In present study, a highly efficient defect-rich graphene/carbon quantum dot composite was successfully obtained and employed as an advanced electrocatalyst for zinc/air batteries. To create more active sites to enhance the performance, one of the most important methods is to form defects in carbon matrix. Other methods are activation process. Here, a defect-rich graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was employed and integrated in the graphene matrix in the first hydrothermal process. Moreover, the following melamine activation during the heat treatment was executed to further fabricate defects on the surface of carbon materials. By innovative combination of the effective two methods, defects-rich carbon composite was synthesized, which lead to an excellent electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction, indicative of great potential in the application of zinc/air batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Defects
Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen reduction reaction
Zinc/air batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of N-GH-GQD-1000 for ORR
N-GH-GQD-1000 as an air cathode in primary zinc/air battery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of N-GH-GQD-1000 (a) and N-GH-1000 (b); TEM images of N-GH-GQD-1000 (c) and N-GH-1000 (d).
Raman spectra of N-GH-GQD-1000 and GH-GQD-1000.

a. The CV curves of N-GH-GQD-1000 measured in O2-saturated and O2-free 0.1 M KOH solution; b. CV curves of Pt/C recorded in O2-saturated and O2-free 0.1 M KOH solution; c. CV curves of N-GH-GQD-1000 and GH-GQD-1000 measured in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution; d. LSV curves of different samples with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1.
a. The discharge voltage profiles of N-GH-GQD-1000 and PtC; b. the discharge curves of N-GH-GQD-1000 and Pt/C at a current density of 50 mA cm−2.
Schematic procedure employed to synthesize the defects-rich graphene/carbon quantum dot composites.

head.ce:title: Defects-rich graphene/carbon quantum dot composites as highly efficient electrocatalysts for aqueous zinc/air batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Environment, Central South University, 410083 Changsha, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of metal-free electrocatalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has great influence on many energy conversion devices. In present study, a highly efficient defect-rich graphene/carbon quantum dot composite was successfully obtained and employed as an advanced electrocatalyst for zinc/air batteries. To create more active sites to enhance the performance, one of the most important methods is to form defects in carbon matrix. Other methods are activation process. Here, a defect-rich graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was employed and integrated in the graphene matrix in the first hydrothermal process. Moreover, the following melamine activation during the heat treatment was executed to further fabricate defects on the surface of carbon materials. By innovative combination of the effective two methods, defects-rich carbon composite was synthesized, which lead to an excellent electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction, indicative of great potential in the application of zinc/air batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: The developments of metal/air batteries and fuel cells are boosted due to the growing demand of energy storage devices with high power density [1,2]. On the basis of this fact, the dynamic process of ORR seems to be a crucial factor in enhancing the electrochemical performance of metal/air batteries [3,4]. To accelerate the ORR process, a highly efficient electrocatalyst is in urgent demand. Although the common Pt-based electrocatalysts possess excellent catalytic activity, the high cost and low reserves restrict its large-scale application [5,6]. Furthermore, the durability of Pt/C in concentrated alkaline solution is not satisfactory [7]. Therefore, developing a suitable and effective cathode material for aqueous metal/air batteries is of great significance.

Carbon material, often known as a highly conductive material with low cost and porous structure, attracts great efforts from researchers. In previous reports, precious metal-free electrocatalyst exhibits satisfactory electrocatalytic activity which makes it become an ideal candidate for noble metal-based catalysts [8,9]. For the sake of creating more rich active sites, a large amount of efforts have been made, such as heteroatom doping [10–12], pores forming [13,14] and defects manufacturing [15,16]. Since the report that N-doped carbon nanotube can perform as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for ORR [17], tremendous attention has been paid to develop a kind of metal-free N doped carbon electrocatalyst [18] for aqueous metal/air batteries. The conventional understanding is that the high content of heteroatom doping can significantly improve the catalytic activity for ORR [19]. However, many reported carbon catalysts with relatively low doping content also achieve famous electrochemical performance. The existed contradiction in the mechanism of heteroatom doping leads researcher to deep investigation [20]. Recently, the influence of defects in the carbon framework has been emphasized. Yi Jia et al. found that even the lower N content can reach higher activity which is contrary to the general N doping theory, suggesting that the defects play an important role in enhancing the catalytic efficiency of carbon material [21]. Graphene, an atom-thick carbon layer, is famous for its electronic conductivity and high surface area. Based on this, graphene is chosen as an optimized carbon matrix as raw materials for carbon electrocatalysts. Furthermore, in previous reports, GQDs were employed to modify the surface of graphene with the purpose of creating defects for the composites to form more active sites for ORR, revealing the enhancement of catalytic activity with the addition of GQDs [22]. In general, until now, melamine activation and GQDs addition were both proved as effective ways to introduce defects on the surface of graphene, which can achieve satisfactory electrocatalytic activity for ORR.

Herein, a defects-rich material was designed by combining GQD modification and N doping. GQD, known as a carbon material with a size of 2–10 nm, can be employed as defective surface modification additive. And the N doping source is melamine which is confirmed to be the activation reagent to create defects on the surface of carbon matrix [21]. On the basis of the performance enhanced function directed by defects, N-doped graphene/carbon quantum dot composite possesses excellent electrocatalytic activity for ORR. The relatively high catalytic activity can be ascribed to the increased amount of defects. Moreover, the ORR activity of as-prepared N-doped graphene/carbon quantum dot composite in different concentration of alkali solution was investigated and further exhibits great application prospect as a high-performance cathode catalyst for zinc/air batteries.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the GH-GQD hydrogel was successfully synthesized by combining GO with graphene quantum dots. Since GQD is a kind of defects-rich material, the surface modification of graphene with GQDs can drastically increase the quantity of defects. The following process is aim to further enrich the defects on the surface of materials. Melamine not only performs as the N doping source but also acts as defects activation reagent. The resultant product was then obtained by heat treatment at relative high temperature 1000 °C.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the SEM image of N-GH-GQD-1000 exhibits a 3D porous graphene material, which is similar with that of N-GH-1000 (Fig. 1b), demonstrating that the addition of GQDs possesses nearly no effect on the obtained morphology during GO gelatinization process. The TEM images of N-GH-GQD-1000 and N-GH-1000 are shown in Fig. 1c and d. It is obvious that the black dots (circled by red circles) existed in the sample N-GH-GQD-1000 which confirms the existence of GQDs. The SEM image of GH-GQD-1000 demonstrates similar microstructure compared to N-GH-GQD-1000, indicating that the melamine cannot change the morphology of the carbon material.

As shown in Fig. S1a, it is obvious that the N 1s peak can hardly be found in the XPS survey spectrum of N-GH-GQD-1000 and demonstrated to be 1.5 at.%. The strong peak at ∼285 eV in both the XPS survey spectrum of N-GH-GQD-1000 and that of GH-GQD-1000 indicate the dominant role of carbon element. The high resolution of C 1s spectra of N-GH-GQD-1000 and GH-GQD-1000 are displayed in Figs. S1b and c. In Fig. S1b, there exist four peaks: a strong peak at 284.80 eV and three weak peaks centered at 285.80 eV, 286.80 eV and 287.75 eV in the C 1s high resolution spectrum of N-GH-GQD-1000 (Fig. S1b). The main peak at 284.80 eV refers to the CC bond inspired by sp2 graphitic carbon structure of the carbon product, while peaks at 285.80 eV, 286.80 eV and 287.75 eV can be assigned to CO/CN, CO and OCO, respectively [23–26]. The C 1s high resolution spectrum of GH-GQD-1000 demonstrates similar carbon contents of CC bond compared with that of N-GH-GQD-1000, indicative of a similar degree of graphitization.

As shown in Fig. 2 , two main peaks can be assigned to D and G band, respectively. The D band and G band is centered at ∼1350 cm−1 and ∼1580 cm−1, respectively. The ID/IG represents for the ratio of intensity for D band and G band. The D and G peaks can both be assigned to the bond stretching of sp2 sites. The G peak mostly stands for stretching mode of all pairs of sp2 atoms in both rings and chains. The D peak is thus ascribed to the breathing modes of sp2 atoms in rings [22]. Two weak peaks centered in 2500 and 2900 cm−1 can be ascribed to 2D and D + G peak [21,27]. The ID/IG of N-GH-GQD-1000 reaches 1.041, while ID/IG of GH-GQD-1000 is 0.9044. This indicates that the defects of N-GH-GQD-1000 are much richer [22,28,29]. On the basis of this, it is noted that the defect-rich N-GH-GQD-1000 can provide more active sites compared with GH-GQD-1000 which can accelerate the oxygen reduction reaction process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of N-GH-GQD-1000 for ORR
ce:section.ce:para: The existence of oxygen reduction process was confirmed by comparing the CV curve measured in O2-free solution with that in O2-saturated solution. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the obvious reduction peak (red line) of N-GH-GQD-1000 centered at −0.13 V can be assigned to ORR. The CV curve of Pt/C displayed in Fig. 4 b demonstrates that too. In Fig. 3c, the increased current of N-GH-GQD-1000 indicates better electronic conductivity compared with GH-GQD-1000 [30]. And the more positive reduction peak of N-GH-GQD-1000 offer the information that N-GH-GQD-1000 possesses a much better catalytic activity compared with GH-GQD-1000. The comparison of LSV curves with several contrast samples was shown in Fig. 3d. The as-prepared N-GH-GQD-1000 exhibits excellent ORR activity over benchmark Pt/C. The increased electrocatalytic performance of N-GH-GQD-1000 compared to N-GH-1000 indicates the effectiveness of GQD which can be ascribed to the rich defects created by GQDs [16]. The better catalytic activity of N-GH-GQD-1000 over GH-GQD-1000 demonstrates that the melamine activation process can favor the oxygen reduction reaction. And 1000 °C is the optimized temperature for the melamine activation process. According to the XPS and Raman results, the N-GH-GQD-1000 possesses relatively low N content, while the defects of N-GH-GQD-1000 are more than that of GH-GQD-1000, revealing that the melamine activation at high temperature can also create defects on the surface of carbon material. Namely, the outstanding performance of N-GH-GQD-1000 over Pt/C can be attributed to the enriched defects created by GQDs and high-temperature treatment with melamine.

The electron transfer process of N-GH-GQD-1000 and GH-GQD-1000 were investigated by calculating the K-L plots at various potentials. As shown in Fig. S2, The K-L plots were derived from the LSV curves of N-GH-GQD-1000 and GH-GQD-1000 at different rotation rates. The electron transfer number of N-GH-GQD-1000 is ∼4 (O2+2H2O+4e− = 4OH−) [31], while that of GH-GQD-1000 comes to be ∼2, indicating that the oxygen reduction route of N-GH-GQD-1000 is much more efficient compared with GH-GQD-1000 [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-GH-GQD-1000 as an air cathode in primary zinc/air battery
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further evaluate the application potential of the carbon material N-GH-GQD-1000, aqueous zinc/air battery was fabricated by employing it as cathode catalyst. PtC loaded air cathode was also assembled to take as comparison. As shown in Fig. 4a, the N-GH-GQD-1000 loaded battery exhibits a much higher discharge voltage at the current density range from 20 to 100 mA cm−2 compared to Pt/C, leading to a higher power density for zinc/air batteries. In Fig. 4b, the long-time discharge curves of N-GH-GQD-1000 and Pt/C are displayed. Furthermore, the stability of N-GH-GQD-1000 during the discharge process is better than that of Pt/C [33], which is satisfactory compared with previous reports in Table S1. These results lead to a conclusion that N-GH-GQD-1000 can offer a great potential in application for zinc/air battery.

The rich defects of the as-prepared N-GH-GQD-1000 were successfully achieved by introducing GQD into the composite and following melamine activation process. Because of the rich defects of N-GH-GQD-1000, it exhibits an excellent catalytic activity for ORR over Pt/C, which makes it become an alternative of noble-metal based catalyst for the application of zinc/air battery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors appreciate the funds offered by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 51674299) and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation funded project (2015M572266). The authors also gratitude to the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (2015cx001), and the Research Innovation Program for College Graduates of Central South University (Grant No. 2016zzts035).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.07.045.
